1: The divine-speech formula vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor (the LORD spoke to Moses, saying) introduces the fourth major offering type. This formulaic opener distinguishes the chattat from the offerings in chs. 1-3: the purification offering is presented as a separate divine command, not a continuation of the previous instructions. The break marks the conceptual distinction between tribute/fellowship offerings (chs. 1-3) and the purgation offering for sin (ch. 4).
2: bishgagah (H7684): 'unintentionally, through inadvertence' — from shagag (to go astray, to err). The chattat (purification offering) covers only sins committed bishgagah, not intentional high-handed transgressions (beyad ramah, Num 15:30-31, for which no sacrifice is available — only divine mercy or death). The distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin is fundamental to the sacrificial system's scope. The chattat is for what was done without full moral intent; it does not mechanically erase premeditated rebellion.
3: ha-kohen ha-mashiach (mashiach, H4899): 'the anointed priest' — the high priest. The same root as meshiach (Messiah/Christ = the anointed one). The anointed priest's unintentional sin is treated with the gravest category of sin-offering (a bull, the most expensive animal) because his sin carries communal consequences: le'ashmat ha-'am — 'bringing guilt on the people.' The leader's moral failure implicates the entire community he represents. This principle of representative contamination applies at every social level in ch. 4 (priest, community, leader, individual).
4: Semicha on the bull's head before the tent of meeting — the same identification gesture as in the 'olah (1:4) and shelamim (3:2,8,13). The laying of hands on the chattat bull formally identifies the offerer with the sacrifice, transferring his sin to the animal. The physical contact transfers not just identity but guilt ('avon) — the animal becomes his representative before YHWH as a guilt-bearer.
5: El ohel mo'ed: the blood of the high priest's chattat is brought inside the tent of meeting — a feature unique to this category of offering. The blood-application at the incense altar inside the tent (v. 7) is the most sacred blood ritual in the regular calendar. The spatial escalation (courtyard altar vs. inner sanctuary) reflects the severity of the sin being addressed: the higher the rank of the sinner, the deeper into the sanctuary the atonement must reach.
6: Vehizel ha-kohen et etzba'o min ha-dam: 'The priest dips his finger in the blood.' Sheva pe'amim: 'seven times' — the seven-fold sprinkling before the parochet (the sanctuary veil). The number seven is the covenant-completion number throughout the OT (seven-day week, seven-year sabbatical, seven-fold sprinkling). The sprinkling before the veil directs the blood toward the Most Holy Place without entering it — the atonement-effect reaches the holiest space through blood-application at its threshold.
7: Qarnot ha-mizbeach (qeren, H7161): 'horns of the altar' — the four projections at the corners of the incense altar. Blood applied to the altar's horns (repeated across multiple offering types) is the most graphic form of blood-application: the horns are the altar's most prominent points, where the power of the sacrifice is concentrated. The pouring of the remainder at the base (el yesod mizbeach ha-'olah) disposes of the blood with reference to the burnt-offering altar — the two altars are connected in the purification ritual.
8: Chelev portions specified for the chattat bull — identical to the shelamim (vv. 8-10 repeat 3:3-5 verbatim). The fat-burning on the altar of burnt offering applies across all offering types where an animal is offered: the best fat always goes to YHWH regardless of the offering's purpose. The fat-theology (v. 3:16 kol chelev la-YHWH) is not suspended for the sin offering; purification and tribute both require giving the best.
9: Kelayot (kidneys) and liver lobe specified identically to the shelamim — the interior organs belong to YHWH even in the purgation offering. The theological claim is consistent: even when the offering is about removing sin rather than celebrating fellowship, the hidden, interior-self organs are YHWH's portion. What is most inward is most holy and most fully YHWH's.
10: 'Just as they are removed from the ox of the shelamim' — the explicit cross-reference to 3:3-5. The identical fat-portion instructions for the chattat and the shelamim signal that the altar's receiving of YHWH's portion is a constant across offering types. The ritual logic is portable: what belongs to YHWH from the fellowship offering belongs to him from the purification offering as well. The divine portion is not renegotiable based on the offering's purpose.
11: 'Or ha-par (the bull's hide) + kol bisaro (all its flesh) — head, legs, entrails, and dung — the entire animal minus the fat portions burned on the altar. The priests receive no portion from the high priest's or the community's chattat bull (unlike individual sin-offerings in 6:26 where the priest eats). The complete disposal of the carcass outside the camp marks the severity of these communal or priestly sin offerings: no benefit returns to any human party.
12: El-mikhon ma'aneh ha-deshen: to the 'clean place' outside the camp where the ashes are disposed of. The entire bull — hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, dung — is carried outside and burned on wood. The burning-outside-the-camp is the complete removal of the sin-material from the covenant community. Its exclusion from the priestly food-system and the camp itself visualizes what atonement accomplishes: the sin-bearing material is expelled and destroyed.
13: Kat ha-'edah kullah ('the entire community') sinning bishgagah — when the corporate body commits an unintentional violation. The extension of the offering system to cover the community as a collective (not just individuals) shows that Israel is conceived as a covenanted corporate entity before YHWH: the community can sin as a body, and the community must make atonement as a body. Venelam davar min ha-'edah — 'the matter was hidden from the assembly' — unintentional community sin by definition involves obscured moral perception.
14: When the communal sin becomes known (nodat hachata't), the assembly brings a par ben baqar (young bull from the herd) — the same animal as the high priest's offering (v. 3). The matched animal for priest and community confirms that the community bears collective guilt equivalent to that of its highest leader. The size of the required offering scales with the scope of the sinner's representative authority.
15: Ziqne ha-'edah (zaqen, H2205): 'the elders of the community.' The elders lay hands on the bull's head on behalf of the assembly — they are the community's representatives in the semicha act. The representative structure of Israel's government (elders representing the people) is formally incorporated into the atonement mechanism: the elders' hands = the community's hands. Corporate guilt is addressed through representative action at every level.
16: The anointed priest (ha-kohen ha-mashiach) takes the blood inside the tent of meeting — the same procedure as for his own chattat (v. 5). The community's sin, like the priest's, requires the blood to reach the inner sanctuary. The spatial reach of the blood-application is determined by the gravity and representational scope of the sin: the more comprehensive the sin, the deeper into the sanctuary the blood must go.
17: Seven-fold sprinkling (sheva pe'amim) before the veil — identical to the procedure for the priest's own offering (v. 6). The communal sin offering uses the identical blood-manipulation technique as the priestly offering. The seven-fold sprinkling before the parochet is the maximum blood-application ritual in the regular calendar (only Yom Kippur adds entry into the Most Holy Place itself).
18: Qarnot ha-mizbeach (horns of the altar) — the incense altar inside the tent. Blood on the horns + remainder poured at the base of the burnt-offering altar (v. 18). The double altar ritual (incense altar inside + burnt offering altar outside) links the two spaces of the tabernacle: the purification reaches both the inner court (where YHWH dwells) and the outer court (where sacrifice is performed). Atonement works from the inside out and from the outside in simultaneously.
19: Ve'et kol chelvo yarim mimenu: 'All its fat he removes.' The fat-burning on the altar — consistent across all offering types. Even in the community's sin offering, where the entire carcass is burned outside the camp (no priestly food-share), the fat portions are first properly burned on the altar. The two disposals happen in order: first the fat to the altar (YHWH's portion); then the remainder to outside the camp (the removal of sin).
20: Vekipper aleihem ha-kohen venislach lahem: 'The priest makes atonement for them, and they will be forgiven.' Kipper (H3722): to make atonement, to cover, to ransom. Salach (H5545): to forgive, to pardon — exclusively used of YHWH's forgiveness of humans in the OT (never of human-to-human forgiveness). The pairing is the standard outcome formula for the chattat: atonement performed by the priest + forgiveness granted by YHWH. The two acts are distinct and sequential: ritual action enables divine response.
21: Chattat ha-qahal: 'the sin offering of the community.' Burned entirely outside the camp, as with the high priest's chattat (v. 12). The outside-the-camp burning marks these two categories (priestly and communal) as the 'whole-consumption' chattat: no one eats any part. The Epistle to the Hebrews (13:11-13) draws on this: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sin offering are burned outside the camp' — and then applies it to Jesus, who suffered 'outside the gate.'
22: Nasi (H5387): 'leader, prince, chief' — the community's civil head (as distinct from the kohen ha-mashiach, the religious head). The term nasi in Ezekiel becomes a key eschatological title for the ideal future leader (Ezek 34:24; 37:25). The nasi's sin offering uses a male goat (se'ir 'izzim, v. 23) rather than a bull — a scaled-down offering reflecting his lesser representative scope compared to the priest or the community as a whole.
23: Se'ir 'izzim (H8163+H5795): 'male goat.' The leader's chattat animal is a male goat — identical to one of the two Yom Kippur goats (Lev 16:9) and the offering for national sin on appointed festivals (Num 28-29). The male goat becomes the standard chattat animal for leaders and communities on regular observances. The gradation of sin-offering animals (bull for priest/community; male goat for leader; female goat or lamb for commoner) reflects the graduated-responsibility principle: higher authority = larger animal = more severe accounting.
24: The leader lays hands on the goat and slaughters it bimqom asher yishchat et ha-'olah lifne YHWH — in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered. The chattat animal for the leader is killed in the same location as the 'olah (north side of the altar, cf. 1:11; 6:25), not in a separate ritual space. The shared slaughter-location across offering types means the ritual geography of the tabernacle courtyard is a fixed, non-negotiable spatial order.
25: Blood on the qarnot mizbeach ha-'olah (horns of the burnt offering altar) — not the incense altar as in the priestly and communal chattat. The leader's sin offering applies blood to the outer burnt-offering altar, not the inner incense altar. The difference marks the lesser gravity: the leader's sin does not require blood to reach the inner sanctuary. The spatial mapping of sin-severity is precise: the deeper the sanctuary penetration, the graver the sin being addressed.
26: Kipper + nislach lo (atonement made + forgiveness granted) — the standard outcome formula for the chattat. The nislach lo ('forgiven to him') is the divine grant of pardon that follows the human ritual of atonement. Throughout Leviticus, forgiveness is never automatic or mechanical: it requires the appropriate ritual action (kipper) but its ultimate granting is YHWH's sovereign act of selach. The two moments (human ritual + divine pardon) are inseparable but distinguishable.
27: Am ha-'aretz (am, H5971 + 'aretz, H776): 'a person of the land' — an ordinary member of the Israelite community, as distinct from the priest, the community leadership, or the nasi. The term later takes on various social/religious connotations in the Second Temple period, but in Leviticus it simply means any ordinary individual Israelite who is not in a leadership role. The offering for the individual commoner is a female animal (goat or lamb) rather than a male, reflecting the smaller scale of individual vs. representative sin.
28: Se'irat 'izzim (H5795+H8163): female goat — the individual's chattat animal for an intentional-class violation. The female animal for the individual's sin offering reflects the gradation principle throughout ch. 4: bull (anointed priest / community) → male goat (leader) → female goat or female lamb (individual). The systematic gradation is not about the animal's monetary value per se but about the representative scope: those who represent more offer more. The individual offers a female animal because they represent only themselves.
29: Semicha, slaughter, and blood at the burnt-offering altar — the same spatial and gestural sequence for the individual's chattat as for the leader's (v. 24-25). The ritual actions are identical across all categories; what differs is the blood-application location (inner vs. outer altar) and the animal used. The structural consistency of the ritual ensures that every level of Israelite society participates in the same system of covenant restoration.
30: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar (mizbeach ha-'olah) with the remainder poured at the base — the same as the leader's offering (v. 25). The individual Israelite's blood ritual matches the leader's in location, confirming that both outer-altar offerings share the same spatial category (as distinguished from the inner-altar offerings of the priest and community). The taxonomy is precise and consistent.
31: Reah nihoah la-YHWH — the pleasing-aroma formula appears for the first time in the chattat chapter at the individual's offering. The earlier chattat variants (priest, community, leader) do not use this formula; the individual's offering does. This may reflect that the fat-burning of the individual's chattat is treated more like the shelamim fat-burning (which feeds the priestly family rather than being entirely consumed). The kipper + nislach lo outcome formula closes the individual female-goat offering.
32: Kivsa (H3775 female): 'a ewe-lamb' — the alternative animal for the individual's chattat. The option of either a female goat or a ewe-lamb provides economic flexibility within the individual-offering category. Both animals are female and must be temimah (without defect). The two-animal option (goat or lamb) for the commoner's sin offering means that the individual's access to the atonement system is the most flexible in the entire ch. 4 gradation.
33: The ewe-lamb's chattat ritual is performed linetach ha-'olah — beside the burnt-offering location. The spatial marker repeated from v. 29 and v. 24 confirms the fixed geography: the leader's and individual's sin offerings are both performed at or near the burnt-offering location (north side of the outer altar), not in front of the veil. The spatial distinction separates the priestly/communal category from the leader/individual category throughout ch. 4.
34: Blood applied to the qarnot mizbeach ha-'olah — identical to the female-goat procedure (v. 30). The lamb and goat procedures for the individual chattat are structurally identical: same animal-sex category, same spatial location, same blood application point. The two animal options differ only in species; the ritual procedure is the same. The systematic parallelism throughout ch. 4 creates a graduated but unified system: one ritual logic, five implementation levels (priest, community, leader, individual-goat, individual-lamb).
35: Kipper alav ha-kohen al chata'to asher chata venislach lo: 'The priest makes atonement for the sin he committed, and he will be forgiven.' The chapter closes with the same outcome formula that appears at each stage (vv. 20, 26, 31) — kipper + nislach. The repetition of this paired formula across all four social categories (priest, community, leader, individual) is programmatic: the entire chattat system exists to make this outcome available to everyone in Israel, from the highest priest to the lowest commoner, through properly executed ritual atonement and YHWH's sovereign grant of pardon.
